chunks.title: Course Description / Special Course Designation
chunks.content:
Overview of the compilation mechanism to generate executable files and
raw binary codes from source codes; executable file formats for an operating
system to run the binary code; disassembly algorithms and control graph
analysis; static and dynamic analyses; case studies on code obfuscation,
codebreaking, malware analysis.

None

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): CSCE 313 or approval of instructor.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
Analyze simple assembly programs to determine their functionality.
Use a debugger to find code that has been packed or encrypted.
Perform dynamic analysis to detect program behavior.
Analyze malware safely in a contained environment.
Understand process injection.
Reverse engineer programs containing simple anti-disassembly and
anti-debugging techniques.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials / Additional Instructional Materials
chunks.content:
This material Is: Required
Practical Malware Analysis
ISBN: 9781593272906
Authors: Michael Sikorski, Andrew Honig
Publisher: No Starch Press
Publication Date: 2012-02-01
URL for Resource:
https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/tamucs/detail.acti
on?docID=1137570
Notes:
Thanks to the support of Texas A&M University
Libraries, you can access this course material
for free at the URL.

This Material is: Required
Laptop
Notes:
Students will need to bring a laptop to class. That laptop should be able to
run Virtual Box and have 50GB of hard drive space available for virtual
machines.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
This course assumes that all work submitted by students will be generated
by the students themselves. Students should not have another person/entity
do the writing of any substantive portion of an assignment for them, which
includes hiring a person or a company to write assignments and using
artificial intelligence tools like ChatGPT.

chunks.title: Grading Policy / Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
The grading scale will be:

A: grade>=90.0
B: 80.0<=grade<90.0
C: 70.0<=grade<80.0
D: 60.0<=grade<70.0
F: grade<60
The overall grade will be determined as follows:
Homework 60%
Final Project 30%
Quizzes 10%
Students enrolled in CSCE 652 are expected to have a more substantive
final project (e.g. involving creation of reusable tools, or a more difficult
malware sample).

Homework challenges may be submitted late until the CTF scoreboard
closes for half credit. Neither quizzes nor the final project may be submitted
late.

chunks.title: Course Summary
chunks.content:
CSCE 652 | Software Reverse Engineering | Fall 2025
Instructor: Martin Carlisle | carlislem@tamu.edu
Meets: TR 12:45PM-2:00PM (LEC), F 3:00PM-5:00PM (LAB) | ZACH 350 (LEC), EABA 118 (LAB)
Topics: compilation mechanism, executable files, raw binary codes, source codes, executable file formats, operating system, disassembly algorithms, control graph analysis, static analysis, dynamic analysis, code obfuscation, codebreaking, malware analysis, assembly programs, debugger, packed code, encrypted code, program behavior, contained environment, process injection, anti-disassembly techniques, anti-debugging techniques, virtualization, x86 Disassembly, Ghidra, C constructs in assembly, Malicious Windows Programs, Debugging, Malware Behavior, Covert Launching, Data Encoding, Network Signatures, Anti-VM, packers, Shellcode, C++, 64-bit malware.
Tools: Virtual Box
Prerequisites: CSCE 313 or approval of instructor
